stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] A client with sickle cell disease presents to the emergency department 5 days after receiving a blood transfusion. The client reports fever and profound weakness, and their hemoglobin is now critically lower than the pre-transfusion level. Based on the electronic health record, complete the bowtie item.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
14:30
S: Client with sickle cell disease is 5 days post-transfusion, presenting with fever, weakness, and dark urine. Hemoglobin has dropped to 4.8 g/dL, which is critically below the pre-transfusion level of 6.2 g/dL.
B: History of HbSS. Received two units of packed red blood cells five days ago for symptomatic anemia.
A: Client is febrile (39.1°C), tachycardic (132 bpm), and hypotensive (90/58 mmHg). Labs confirm severe hemolysis with suppressed reticulocytes (1%), indicating hyperhemolysis syndrome.
R: Avoid all further transfusions. Prepare for immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., IVIG). Immediately notify hematology and the blood bank.

tabs.title: Hematology & Transfusion Log
tabs.content:
Test ItemBaseline (Pre-Transfusion)Today (Post-Transfusion)Reference Range
Hemoglobin (Hgb)6.2 g/dL4.8 g/dL (Critical)13.5-17.5 g/dL (Female)
Reticulocyte Count12%1% (Suppressed)0.5-2.5%
Urine ColorAmberDark/Black (Cola)Yellow/Amber
Indirect Bilirubin2.2 mg/dL8.4 mg/dL (Elevated)0.2-0.8 mg/dL

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:
Time: 14:30
BP: 90/58 mmHg
HR: 132 bpm
RR: 26 breaths/min
Temp: 102.4°F (39.1°C)
SpO2: 93% on room air

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content:
MedicationDosageRouteFrequencyLast Administered
Ondansetron4 mgIVq6h PRN nauseaN/A (Not administered post-transfusion)
Acetaminophen650 mgPOq4h PRN feverN/A (Not administered post-transfusion)

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Appears weak and uncomfortable. Integumentary: Skin is pale with mild jaundice. Dark urine noted. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, S1 and S2 present, no murmurs auscultated. Respiratory: Increased work of breathing, lung sounds clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, normoactive bowel sounds. Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Complains of generalized weakness.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-Ray: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormalities. Lungs are clear. Heart size within normal limits.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Nursing Priorities: 1. Monitor for signs and symptoms of worsening hemolysis. 2. Administer medications as prescribed to manage symptoms and prevent complications. 3. Provide supportive care to promote comfort and prevent secondary infections. Nursing Interventions: 1. Assess vital signs and oxygen saturation every 2 hours. 2. Monitor urine output and color every hour. 3. Administer IV fluids as prescribed to maintain hydration. 4. Implement infection control measures, including hand hygiene and sterile technique.

conditions.c1: Incorrect. While DHTR involves hemolysis, it typically causes hemoglobin to return to baseline, not drop critically below it. The profound bone marrow suppression (reticulocytopenia) is a hallmark of HHS, not a typical DHTR.
conditions.c2: Correct. Hyperhemolysis syndrome involves autoimmune destruction of both donor and the client's own red blood cells. This pathophysiology explains the paradoxical drop in hemoglobin below pre-transfusion levels and the suppressed bone marrow response.
conditions.c3: Incorrect. Iron overload is a chronic condition resulting from multiple transfusions over years. This client's presentation is acute and involves massive hemolysis, which is inconsistent with transfusional hemosiderosis.

actions.a1: Correct. Further transfusions provide more antigens that fuel the autoimmune process in HHS, leading to accelerated hemolysis. The priority is to stop this cycle by withholding blood products.
actions.a2: Correct. Since HHS is an immune-mediated process, immunosuppressive agents like IVIG or corticosteroids are required. These therapies work to halt the autoimmune destruction of red blood cells.
actions.a3: Incorrect. This action is contraindicated and extremely dangerous. Administering more blood would intensify the hemolytic process, causing a further drop in hemoglobin and risking multi-organ failure.
actions.a4: Incorrect. The anemia is caused by acute hemolysis, not an iron deficiency. Administering iron is clinically irrelevant to this life-threatening autoimmune process and would not be effective.

parameters.p1: Correct. The hemoglobin level tracks the severity of the anemia and response to treatment. The reticulocyte count is a crucial indicator of bone marrow recovery; a rising count signals that hemolysis is resolving.
parameters.p2: Correct. The presence of dark, cola-colored urine is a direct sign of hemoglobinuria from ongoing intravascular hemolysis. The urine clearing is a key visual indicator that the hemolytic process is subsiding.
parameters.p3: Incorrect. While corticosteroids may affect blood glucose, this is a secondary concern. Monitoring glucose does not provide direct information about the resolution of the life-threatening hemolysis.
parameters.p4: Incorrect. Trousseau's sign is used to assess for hypocalcemia. This finding is not directly related to the pathophysiology or primary complications of hyperhemolysis syndrome.

pearls.description: Hyperhemolysis syndrome (HHS) is a life-threatening complication post-transfusion in sickle cell disease, characterized by a rapid drop in hemoglobin below pre-transfusion levels.
pearls.strategy: Early recognition and prompt intervention with immunosuppressive therapy are crucial to prevent severe morbidity and mortality.

pearls.description: Suppressed reticulocyte count in the setting of acute anemia post-transfusion is a key indicator of HHS, distinguishing it from other hemolytic transfusion reactions.
pearls.strategy: Always correlate lab findings with clinical presentation to guide appropriate management decisions.

traps.description: Assuming that all post-transfusion anemia is due to simple delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR) can lead to inappropriate management.
traps.strategy: Differentiate HHS from DHTR by assessing the reticulocyte count and the degree of hemoglobin drop. HHS requires immunosuppression, while DHTR may not.

traps.description: Administering more blood transfusions in HHS will exacerbate the hemolytic process and worsen the patient's condition.
traps.strategy: Withhold further transfusions and initiate immunosuppressive therapy immediately upon suspicion of HHS.

mnemonics.description: Suppressed Retics, Transfusion Reaction, Oxygenation compromised, Prepare Immunosuppression, Hemoglobin dropping, Halt Transfusions, Seek Expert Help
